listitem.para: If /etc/subuid exists, the commands useradd and newusers (unless the user already have subordinate user IDs) allocate SUB_UID_COUNT unused user IDs from the range SUB_UID_MIN to SUB_UID_MAX for each new user.
The default values for SUB_UID_MIN, SUB_UID_MAX, SUB_UID_COUNT are respectively 100000, 600100000 and 65536.

If set to yes, the commands useradd, usermod, and newusers will calculate subordinate UID ranges deterministically based on the user's UID instead of searching for the next free range. The formula used is:
listitem.literallayout: start = SUB_UID_MIN + ((UID - UID_MIN) * SUB_UID_COUNT) end = start + SUB_UID_COUNT - 1
listitem.para: This ensures the same UID always receives the same subordinate UID range on every system, making it suitable for environments with centralized user management (LDAP, NIS, etc.) or synchronized UIDs across systems.
If SUB_UID_DETERMINISTIC is enabled, you can use usermod --add-subuids -S to produce deterministic subuids.

WARNING: Because UID_MIN is used to calculate the ranges, any change of UID_MIN will disrupt the algorithm. UID_MAX is used to calculate the ranges, any change of UID_MAX will disrupt the algorithm. Users with identities less than UID_MIN are incompatible with SUB_UID_DETERMINISTIC, but can still be set manually.

WARNING: Do not mix deterministic and linear (default) allocation on the same system or across systems sharing /etc/subuid via network storage (NFS, etc.). Mixing allocation methods
will cause subordinate ID range conflicts and overlaps .
The default value for SUB_UID_DETERMINISTIC is no.

refsect2.title: Subordinate ID Space Planning
refsect2.para: When planning subordinate ID allocation, calculate the maximum number of users the space can accommodate:
For high-density environments with many users and smaller allocations:

para.emphasis: WARNING: SECURITY RISK - MAY CAUSE RANGE OVERLAPS AND PRIVILEGE ESCALATION!

listitem.para: Only effective when SUB_UID_DETERMINISTIC is set to yes.

When set to yes (WRAP MODE), allows the deterministic range calculation to wrap around using modulo arithmetic when a UID would overflow the configured subordinate ID space. The subordinate ID space is treated as a ring buffer.

WARNING: Range overlaps can lead to container escapes and privilege escalation. For example, with SUB_UID_MIN=100000, SUB_UID_MAX=200000, SUB_UID_COUNT=65536, User A (UID 1000) gets range [100000, 165535] and User B (UID 1001) wraps and overlaps with User A. User B's container can now access files from User A's containers.
Use only in development, testing, or tightly constrained lab environments.
When set to no (default), any arithmetic overflow or range exceeding SUB_UID_MAX is a hard error. This guarantees non-overlapping, monotonic allocation.
The default value for UNSAFE_SUB_UID_DETERMINISTIC_WRAP is no.
